ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for White Channa Chaat; closest parent is chickpea food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chaat preparations; A0CKP (Chickpeas, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for White Channa Chaat or chickpea chaat as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of White Channa Chaat is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one restaurant plate as typically served in Indian eateries in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chickpea (Bengal gram, mature seeds, boiled) and chaat component estimation based on ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chickpeas, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (NDB 16057)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: White Channa Chaat is a mixed dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for cooked chickpeas and chaat components (onion, tomato, chutneys, spices). USDA SR Legacy cooked chickpea data used for cross-validation. The dish is predominantly chickpea-based (~55–60% cooked chickpeas by weight), with the remainder being vegetables, chutneys, and spices. Values represent a composite estimate for the assembled dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chickpeas, boiled (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: White Channa Chaat is a chickpea-dominant dish. GI of 28 is based on boiled chickpeas (kabuli chana) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The addition of tamarind chutney, lemon juice, and vegetables does not materially raise the GI above the chickpea baseline. GL per serving = (28 × 22.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 15.4 (based on 250 g serving, 22.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from chaat masala, tamarind chutney, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: White Channa Chaat contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (800 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and chutney portions or limit serving size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 28) and high dietary fibre from chickpeas
type2_diabetes.guidance: White Channa Chaat has a low GI of 28 and provides 5.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, both of which support postprandial glycaemic control; a standard 250 g serving yields a glycaemic load of approximately 15, which is within acceptable range for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat, and plant-based protein from chickpeas
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: White Channa Chaat is low in saturated fat (0.3 g per 100 g) and cholesterol-free, with high soluble fibre from chickpeas that supports LDL cholesterol reduction; however, the sodium content from chutneys and spice mixes warrants portion moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Channa Chaat (also spelled White Chana Chaat or Safed Chana Chaat) is a popular Indian street food and snack dish made primarily from boiled white chickpeas (kabuli chana), dressed with a vibrant combination of finely chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, fresh coriander, tamarind chutney, green mint-coriander chutney, chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, lemon juice, and salt. The dish is typically served at room temperature or slightly chilled, offering a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously tangy, spicy, savoury, and slightly sweet from the tamarind. The texture contrasts soft, yielding chickpeas with the crunch of raw onion and tomato. White Channa Chaat is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly suitable across dietary preferences. It is a nutritionally dense dish: chickpeas provide substantial plant-based protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g), high dietary fibre (5.2 g per 100 g), folate, iron, and magnesium. The low glycaemic index (GI 28) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The primary clinical concern is sodium from chutneys and chaat masala, which can reach 800 mg per standard serving. In Singapore, White Channa Chaat is served in Indian restaurants and casual dining establishments, often as a starter or light meal. It is closely related to other chaat preparations such as Aloo Chaat and Papdi Chaat but is distinguished by its exclusive use of white chickpeas without fried elements.
common_user_queries: How many calories in white chana chaat?
Is channa chaat good for diabetics?
What is the GI of chickpea chaat?
Is white chana chaat gluten free?
How much protein in channa chaat?
Is chana chaat good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for White Channa Chaat exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked chickpea data and USDA SR Legacy, with component-based estimation for the chaat dressing. GI is based on the well-documented boiled chickpea GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite nature of the dish and restaurant-level variation in chutney and spice quantities.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates for the assembled dish, not a single-source exact match. The chickpea base (~55-60% by weight) is anchored to ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy cooked chickpea values; dressing components (onion, tomato, chutneys, spices) are estimated from ICMR-NIN vegetable and condiment tables. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — chickpeas and vegetable-based dressings contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish; B12 is not present in plant foods). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal or fortified ingredients; plant-based dish). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component data. 7. Sodium estimate of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with tamarind chutney, chaat masala, and added salt; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 8. GI of 28 is based on boiled kabuli chickpeas per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). GL per 250 g serving = (28 × 22.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 15.4. 9. The food_name has been corrected from 'White Channa Chat' (input) to 'White Channa Chaat' (canonical spelling); 'White Channa Chat' is retained as an alias. 10. This dish is Indian in origin and served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
